---
title: "Study Design and Analysis Guidelines for Genomic Scale Data"
subtitle: "Laying down the foundation for reproducible findings"
author: ""
date: ""
output:
  revealjs::revealjs_presentation:
    theme: night
    highlight: pygments
    center: false
    transition: slide
    slide_level: 1
    self_contained: true
    css: |
      .reveal h1 { font-size: 1.2em; color: #4ec9b0; }
      .reveal h2 { font-size: 1.0em; color: #9cdcfe; }
      .reveal ul { font-size: 0.65em; }
      .reveal p  { font-size: 0.65em; }
      .reveal table { font-size: 0.65em; }
      .reveal .small { font-size: 0.60em; color: #ccc; }
      .reveal .highlight-box {
        background: rgba(78,201,176,0.12);
        border-left: 4px solid #4ec9b0;
        padding: 0.4em 0.8em;
        margin: 0.5em 0;
        border-radius: 4px;
      }
      .reveal .warn-box {
        background: rgba(255,180,0,0.12);
        border-left: 4px solid #ffb400;
        padding: 0.4em 0.8em;
        margin: 0.5em 0;
        border-radius: 4px;
      }
---

# Study Design and Analysis Guidelines for Genomic Scale Data {data-background="#1a1a2e"}

<br>

> **Laying down the foundation for reproducible findings**

---

# Evolution of Translational Omics (ETO)

**Lessons Learned and the Path Forward** — *Institute of Medicine*

<div class="highlight-box">
**"Omics"** encompasses multiple molecular disciplines involving characterization of global sets of biological molecules: DNAs, RNAs, proteins, and metabolites.  
Key feature: **many more variables than analysis units**.
</div>

- **ETO**: Recommendations for a discovery and development process to usher omics-based tests into the clinical setting
- Guidelines are intended to **protect the public** from dangers of flawed tests
- Includes **information dissemination guidelines** to promote transparency
- Adopting these guidelines will help our internal development process: avoid propagation of flawed results and catch errors early
- Where ETO refers to an *Omics-based test*, replace with *Omics-based results* — the building blocks of eventual tests

---

# Omics-based Test Development Process

<br>

```
Discovery Phase
    └── Candidate Model Development (Training Data)
            └── Lock-Down of Analytical & Computational Procedures
                    └── Evaluation of Candidate Omics-Based Test
                            ├── Independent Sample Set (preferred)
                            └── Subset of Training Set NOT used in training (less preferred)
```

<div class="warn-box">
⚠️ **All Validation Sets are Not Equal!** At lock-down, the evaluation criteria must be specified and locked.
</div>

---

# Omics-based Model Development – Discovery Phase

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **1. Training Data** | Candidate model developed on training data with fit set / test set or cross-validation |
| **2. Lock-Down** | Lock model and evaluation procedure |
| **3. Evaluate** | Use independent sample set (preferred) or held-out training subset |
| **4. Adjust** | If model requires adjustments, a **new** evaluation set is required |

<div class="highlight-box">
Estimated Performance Level should be **High**, and **Accurate!**  
Simple enough — what could possibly go wrong?
</div>

---

# Threats to Validity of Results

**ASCO 2012** — David F. Ransohoff, MD:

> *"Bias is the main problem in non-randomized studies"*

**Strength of Science: Do we trust the results?**

Threats to validity include:

- Weak or ill-specified **question**
- Weak **method**
- **Design** — biased or under-powered
- **Overfitting** — analysis produces "chance" results

<div class="warn-box">
**Bias** = systematic (non-biological) differences between sample sets being compared.  
When these biases exist, observed effects cannot be attributed to biology.
</div>

---

# Bias Example 1 — Ovarian Cancer Proteomics

*The Lancet, Vol. 359, No. 9306. (Feb 2002)*

- Training set: mass spectroscopy data from **50 unaffected** women and **50 ovarian cancer** patients
- Applied blinded to 50 cancers and 66 unaffected cases
- **Sensitivity = 100%, Specificity = 95%** using Fit Set/Test Set — but results were **not reproducible**

**What went wrong?**

<div class="warn-box">
Mass spec measurements for cancer and non-cancer cases were done on **different days**, *prior* to allocation to Fit Set or Test Set.  
→ These were **not independent** data sets.
</div>

- K. Baggerly "discovered" this by looking at the chip numbers
- Took **a year** to figure it out
- Full disclosure of methods would have made this problem readily apparent

---

# Bias Example 2 — Prostate Cancer Serum Peptidome

*The Journal of Clinical Investigation, Vol. 116, No. 1. (Jan 2006)*

**Results:** Peptide patterns are **100% sensitive and specific** to Prostate cancer

| Group | Mean Age | Gender |
|-------|----------|--------|
| **Cancer** | 67 | 100% Male |
| **Control** | 35 | 42% Male |

<div class="warn-box">
Confounding variables (age, gender) were not balanced across groups.  
The "signal" may be demographic, not biological.
</div>

---

# Bias Example 3 — GHI Colon Polyps

**Sample Tissue Block Descriptions**

| Year | Benign | Cancerous |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 2008 | 60 | 8 |
| 2007 | 1 | 48 |

<div class="warn-box">
In studies based on **archival specimens** from tissue banks or hospitals — how can we exercise more control over the sample selection process?
</div>

**Full picture:**

| Set 1 Year | Benign | Cancerous | Set 2 Year | Benign | Cancerous |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| 2007 | 1 | 48 | ≤ 2002 | 53 | 7 |
| 2008 | 60 | 8 | > 2002 | 1 | 42 |

---

# Bias Example 4 — Affymetrix Genechip Profiles

**Setup:** Several hundred breast tumor samples hybridized to early Affymetrix Genechips

- Unsupervised clustering → **2 very distinct clusters**
- Gene signatures driving the clusters were **not interpretable**

**Possible explanation:**

<div class="warn-box">
For 2 weeks prior to year-end, production ran **7 days/week** to meet bonus goals.  
The two clusters corresponded to samples processed on **two consecutive weekends**.
</div>

*"The problem with your manufacturing process is your management."* — Deming to Ford

---

# Batch Effects: A Significant Threat to Omics Validity

- Batch effects on **microarray data** have been well documented
- Batch effects in **RNASeq data** are also being addressed
- These can be mitigated by **proper sample processing designs**
- Batch effects may be an **even bigger threat** in mutation profiling:
  - Patterns of sequencing errors found in one study (Nakamura 2011) not observed in next (Minoche 2011)
  - Suggests including **several independent sequencing datasets** in analysis

**The plethora of methods is astounding:**  
GATK, Samtools, VarScan, SNVMix, SERVIC4E, CrossSampleErrEst, SNVer, CRISP, Atlas-SNP2, and many more

<div class="highlight-box">
The key to valid, generalizable results: **sound sample selection**, careful processing, and careful data analysis to guard against overfitting.
</div>

---

# ETO: Steps to Ensure Validity of Results

## 1. Data Quality Assessment
- Crucial first step — includes general and application-specific assessments
- A **data quality report** must be produced and examined before proceeding
- QA is an *active, engaging process* — not just a routine to check off

## 2. Computational Model Development
- **Overfitting** is a major concern: performance will be great on training set, but poor on other samples

## 3. Confirmation on an Independent Evaluation Dataset
- A computational model's ultimate utility is measured by its **performance on future samples**

## 4. Documentation
- Data and fully specified computational procedures should be stored in a **protected area under version control**

---

# Training–Evaluation Set Separation

- Evaluation set should be **representative of future samples** (tissue type, stage, etc.) and ideally **fully independent** of the Training set
- When not possible, split dataset into Training and Validation subsets **at random**
- Some mutation analysis developers split by **genomic region** — this is the **least desirable** split

**Avoiding Overfitting:**

- Training set can be randomly split into Fit set and Test set, or **cross-validation** can be used
- Test set / CV performance assessments are usually **optimistic**
- CV estimates can suffer from **bias and high-variance** *(Elements of Statistical Learning)*

<div class="warn-box">
Fit and test sets share similarities that may not be shared by future samples processed later in time or from different populations.
</div>

---

# ETO – Best Practices for the Discovery Phase

<div class="highlight-box">
**Confirm results on one or more independent sets of samples**  
*Oncotype DX example*: Recurrence Score validated using multiple independent datasets (Paik et al., 2004) from different trials at multiple institutions.
</div>

- **Data used for analyses** should be documented and made available

- **Computer code and fully specified computational procedures** should be documented and made available

- **Omics-based test or procedure** should be defined precisely, including:
  - Lab protocols
  - Computational procedures
  - Intended uses

---

# Summary: Laying Down the Foundation

- Recommendations come from **basic scientific principles** and are fairly obvious
- As we strive to accelerate development, we incur higher risks of errors — we should simultaneously make the process **more robust and rigorous**
- Most errors or flawed results are based on **faulty assumptions**
- It is **Good Quality Data** and **Careful Data Analysis** that is going to save the day
- It is **everybody's responsibility** to ensure that sensible methods are used

<div class="warn-box">
"Maybe it is because these principles are so basic that they are easily and often ignored?"
</div>

---

# Emerging Omics Data Analysis Techniques

<br>

<div class="highlight-box">
**Extremely high data dimensionality** gives rise to extreme risk of **overfitting** computational models.
</div>

<br>

> Analyzing several **independent sequencing data sets** is crucial to overcoming these risks.

---

# "Personalized" Oncology: Biomarkers as Drugs

**Daniel F. Hayes, MD:**

> *"A bad tumor marker is as harmful as a bad drug!"*

**Undervalue of Tumor Biomarkers — a Vicious Cycle:**

```
Marker Utility is Poorly Valued
  → Low Funding for Research
    → Low Level of Evidence
      → Higher Skepticism & Few Recommendations for Clinical Use
        → Marker Utility is Poorly Valued ...
```

**Way out of the vicious cycle:**

- Studies of convenience: *"I have lunch with a colleague, I happen to have samples in a freezer…"* — 98% of BM discovery studies *(ok to generate hypotheses — sometimes)*
- Apply the **same level of evidence** required for drugs to tumor biomarker discovery: design a **prospective study** with protocol and analysis plan

---

# TMUGS: Levels of Evidence

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **I** | Prospectively designed studies with written protocol — Marker is **Primary Objective** |
| **II** | Prospective — Marker is **Secondary Objective** *(Most Tumor Marker Studies)* |
| **III** | Retrospective, Outcomes, **Multivariate** Analysis |
| **IV** | Retrospective, Outcomes, **Univariate** Analysis |
| **V** | Retrospective, Correlation with Other Marker, **No Outcomes** |

<div class="highlight-box">
The **prospective-retrospective design** using archived specimens offers a practical middle ground.
</div>

---

# Sample Selection and Handling

**(Un)Common Sense Examples**

- Tests should be developed on samples **properly selected from the population** in which the test will be applied

- **Sample processing** — order, machine, lane, etc. — should be fully specified and optimized where possible

- When data are used to **tune** how the data are manipulated to arrive at test results, those data **cannot be used to evaluate** how the test will perform with other data

---

# References

<div class="small">

- Baggerly, K.A. et al. (2004). Reproducibility of SELDI-TOF protein patterns in serum. *Bioinformatics*, 20(5):777–785.
- Baggerly, K.A. et al. (2009). Deriving Chemosensitivity from Cell Lines: Forensic Bioinformatics. *Annals of Applied Statistics*, 3(4):1309–1334.
- Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R. and Friedman, J. (2009). *The Elements of Statistical Learning*, Springer, 2nd ed.
- Hayes, D.F. et al. (1996). Tumor marker utility grading system. *J. Natl. Cancer Inst.*, 88:1456–1466.
- Micheel, C.M., Nass, S.J. and Omenn, G.S. (2012). *Evolution of Translational Omics*. Institute of Medicine.
- Petricoin, E.F. et al. (2002). Use of proteomic patterns in serum to identify ovarian cancer. *The Lancet*, 359(9306):572–577.
- Simon, R. et al. (2003). Pitfalls in the Use of DNA Microarray Data. *JNCI*, 95(1):14–18.
- Varma, S. and Simon, R. (2006). Bias in error estimation when using cross-validation. *BMC Bioinformatics*, 7:91.
- Villanueva, J. et al. (2006). Differential exoprotease activities confer tumor-specific serum peptidome patterns. *JCI*, 116(1):271–284.

**Batch Effects:**

- Leek, J.T. et al. (2010). Tackling batch effects in high-throughput data. *Nat Rev Genet*, 11(10):733–739.
- Gagnon-Bartsch & Speed (2012). Using control genes to correct for unwanted variation. *Biostatistics*, 13(3):539–52.
- Auer, P.L. & Doerge, R.W. (2010). Statistical Design and Analysis of RNA Sequencing Data. *Genetics*.

</div>






<!-- To run
# nohup Rscript -e "knitr::knit2html('OmicsTests.Rmd')" > OmicsTests.log  &

# Or
# nohup Rscript -e "rmarkdown::render('OmicsTests.Rmd')" > OmicsTests.log  &

-->
